senses.definition: Plural of aedeagus: the male reproductive organ in many insects and other arthropods that delivers sperm during mating.
examples: The researcher compared the shapes of the aedeagi to tell the beetle species apart.
In many insect groups, the aedeagi are hardened and often very complex structures.
Taxonomists often dissect male specimens to examine their aedeagi under a microscope.

senses.definition: The male reproductive organ of an insect or other arthropod, used to transfer sperm to the female during mating.
examples: The aedeagus is attached to the male insect’s abdomen and is used to deliver sperm to the female.
The entomologist removed the aedeagus to study its internal structure.
Differences in the aedeagus can help scientists identify closely related beetle species.

senses.definition: An alternative plural form of aedeagus, referring to the male reproductive organs of insects and other arthropods.
examples: Minute differences in the aedegi allowed the team to separate the two fly species.
The guidebook includes detailed drawings of the aedegi of common beetles.

senses.definition: A genus of mosquitoes that includes several species which spread diseases such as dengue, Zika, and yellow fever.
examples: Aedes mosquitoes are responsible for transmitting dengue fever in many tropical regions.
Public health campaigns often focus on controlling Aedes larvae in standing water.
Researchers studied Aedes populations in urban areas to predict disease risk.

senses.definition: In ancient Roman religion and classical architecture, a small shrine or niche, often shaped like a tiny temple, that holds a statue or image of a god.
examples: An aedicula in the villa’s wall contained a small statue of a household deity.
The church façade is decorated with an aedicula above the main door.
In Roman houses, an aedicula might serve as a sacred place for family offerings.

senses.definition: Plural of aedicula: small architectural shrines or niches, often temple-shaped, used to frame statues, paintings, or urns.
examples: The façade is lined with aediculae containing statues of saints.
In the catacombs, aediculae sometimes held the funeral urns of early Christians.

senses.definition: An English form of aedicula: a small shrine or architectural niche, usually with columns and a pediment, that frames a statue or other object.
examples: The altar is set within an elaborate aedicule of marble columns.
Each window is topped by a Renaissance-style aedicule.

senses.definition: Plural of aedicule: small shrine-like or niche-like architectural features that often surround statues or images.
examples: Aedicules along the arcade create rhythm on the building’s front.
The designer added aedicules above the doors to echo classical motifs.

senses.definition: In ancient Rome, an elected official responsible for public buildings, markets, games, and the grain and water supply of the city.
examples: As aedile, he organized the public games to win the support of the people.
The aedile inspected the markets to ensure fair prices and good quality goods.
A successful year as aedile could boost a politician’s chances of higher office.

senses.definition: Plural of aedile: the Roman officials who looked after public buildings, games, and supplies such as grain and water.
examples: Two plebeian aediles and two curule aediles shared the city’s responsibilities.
The aediles competed to provide the most impressive public festivals.

senses.definition: The office, period of service, or rank of an aedile in ancient Rome.
examples: His aedileship was marked by generous public games and building projects.
Holding the aedileship was an important step in the Roman political career path.

senses.definition: Plural of aedileship: different terms of office held by one or more aediles in ancient Rome.
examples: The historian compared the aedileships of several politicians in the late Republic.
Lavish spending during their aedileships often left ambitious Romans in debt.

senses.definition: Relating to an aedile or to the office and duties of an aedile in ancient Rome.
examples: The aedilian duties included supervising markets and public games.
He studied aedilian reforms in the late Republican period.

senses.definition: Concerning an aedile or the functions associated with an aedile in Roman public life.
examples: Aedilic authority extended over markets and public buildings.
The statute set out the aedilic regulations for grain distribution.

senses.definition: Relating to the aediles or their office in ancient Rome.
examples: The law listed various aedilitian obligations concerning public games.
He wrote a detailed study of aedilitian supervision of markets.

senses.definition: Plural of aedility: the offices or roles of aediles in different places or times.
examples: Municipal aedilities in the provinces copied those of Rome.
The historian compared local aedilities across Italy.

senses.definition: The position or function of an aedile in ancient Rome.
examples: Aedility was part of the traditional sequence of public offices.
He entered aedility after serving as quaestor.

senses.definition: Relating to or characteristic of mosquitoes of the genus Aedes.
examples: Aedine mosquitoes are important vectors of several human diseases.
The study focused on aedine populations in suburban areas.

senses.definition: Alternative plural of aedoeagus, a variant term for the male reproductive organ of an insect.
examples: The aedoeagi of related species often differ in subtle ways.
Drawings of the aedoeagi are included in the taxonomic key.

senses.definition: A variant form of aedeagus, meaning the male genital organ of an insect that transfers sperm during mating.
examples: The aedoeagus is often hardened and species-specific in beetles.
Damage to the aedoeagus can prevent successful mating.

senses.definition: The scientific or scholarly study of the genitals, especially the sexual organs of animals.
examples: His early work in aedoeology focused on the reproductive systems of insects.
Aedoeology can shed light on mating behavior and species evolution.

senses.definition: In Scots, honest, faithful, and single-minded in feeling or support.
examples: He remained an aefald friend through all their troubles.
The poem praises her aefald love and loyalty.

senses.definition: The quality of being honest, faithful, and single-minded in your support or feelings.
examples: Her aefaldness won the respect of everyone in the community.
The letter is signed with a phrase expressing aefaldness and sincerity.

senses.definition: In a faithful and sincere way; with single-hearted loyalty.
examples: He served his master aefaldy all his life.
She wrote, aefaldy assuring them of her support.

senses.definition: Scots form of aefald: faithful, sincere, and single-minded.
examples: He was known as an aefauld companion in every hardship.
The oath was taken aefauld and true.

senses.definition: Plural of aegagrus: wild goats, especially those found in mountainous regions.
examples: Hunters in the highlands sometimes caught aegagri for meat and hides.
Ancient authors described aegagri living on steep sea cliffs.

senses.definition: A rounded ball formed from plant fibers, hair, or other material found in the stomach of some animals, or washed up in lakes or seas.
examples: The shepherd found an aegagropila in the goat’s stomach during the autopsy.
Strange aegagropilae lay along the shore like small brown balls.

senses.definition: Plural of aegagropila: balls of hair or plant material from animal stomachs or from bodies of water.
examples: Several aegagropilae were discovered in the dissected animal.
The beach was dotted with dark aegagropilae after the storm.

senses.definition: Another form of aegagropila, meaning a ball of vegetable fibers or hair found in animal stomachs or in water.
examples: The goat’s aegagropile was preserved in the museum collection.
An aegagropile formed over time from undigested plant fibers.

senses.definition: Plural of aegagropile: multiple balls of hair or plant fibers from animal stomachs or bodies of water.
examples: The scientist studied aegagropiles formed in the stomachs of grazing animals.
On the shore, aegagropiles collected among the driftwood.

senses.definition: A wild goat, especially one found in rocky or mountainous areas.
examples: Ancient writers sometimes mentioned the aegagrus in their descriptions of mountain wildlife.
The aegagrus is well adapted to steep and stony terrain.

senses.definition: Relating to the Aegean Sea or the regions and islands around it.
examples: They spent their holiday sailing among the Aegean islands.
The museum has a rich collection of Aegean pottery.
Warm Aegean winds blew across the coast in the evening.

senses.definition: The Aegean Sea, the part of the Mediterranean Sea between Greece and Turkey.
examples: The Aegean lies between the Greek mainland and western Turkey.
Many ancient trade routes crossed the Aegean.

senses.definition: A rare or obsolete form of hegemony, meaning leadership or dominance, especially of one state or group over others.
examples: Writers once spoke of the city’s aegemony in the region.
The kingdom’s aegemony gradually weakened over the centuries.

senses.definition: In Latin or Latin-based contexts, sick or ill.
examples: The phrase habeas corpus aeger refers to a writ concerning a sick prisoner.
In old medical notes, the patient is sometimes simply marked aeger.

senses.definition: Relating to moths of the genus Aegeria or the family Aegeriidae, often called clearwing moths.
examples: Aegerian larvae can be serious pests of fruit trees.
The aegerian species are known for their wasp-like appearance.

senses.definition: A moth belonging to the family Aegeriidae, commonly known as clearwing moths.
examples: The peach tree was damaged by an aegeriid boring into its trunk.
Several aegeriids mimic the appearance of wasps.

senses.definition: A family of moths commonly called clearwing moths, whose larvae often bore into plant stems or trunks.
examples: Aegeriidae include several important orchard pests.
The clear wings of Aegeriidae make them resemble wasps.

senses.definition: A former genus name for certain small shorebirds, now usually placed in other genera such as Charadrius.
examples: Older field guides still list some plovers under Aegialitis.
Taxonomists later reassigned Aegialitis species to other genera.

senses.definition: An old medical term for a severe headache or migraine affecting one side of the head.
examples: The physician diagnosed the patient with aegicrania in the right temple.
Aegicrania kept him in a dark room for most of the day.

senses.definition: A genus of wild grasses related to wheat, some species of which hybridize with cultivated wheat.
examples: Aegilops species can pass useful genes to wheat through breeding.
The field margins were thick with Aegilops and other wild grasses.

senses.definition: A Greek island in the Saronic Gulf, near Athens.
examples: They took a ferry from Athens to Aegina for a day trip.
Aegina is known for its pistachios and ancient temple ruins.

senses.definition: Relating to the island of Aegina or its people.
examples: Aeginetan pottery was traded widely in the ancient Aegean.
The Aeginetan sailors were famous in classical times.

senses.definition: A person from the island of Aegina.
examples: An Aeginetan guided them around the island’s ancient sites.
The poet mentions an Aeginetan among the group of travelers.

senses.definition: Relating to Aegina, its people, or its style of art, especially in ancient times.
examples: The sculptor worked in an Aeginetic style influenced by early Greek art.
Aeginetic artists were known for their strong, simple forms.

senses.definition: In Greek mythology, a goat-like or goat-fish deity often identified with Pan and associated with the constellation Capricorn.
examples: According to the myth, Aegipan helped Zeus and was placed among the stars.
On ancient vases, Aegipan sometimes appears with a goat’s body and a fish’s tail.

senses.definition: In Norse mythology, a powerful sea being or giant who personifies the ocean and hosts feasts for the gods.
examples: In the sagas, Aegir brews ale for the gods in his great hall under the sea.
Sailors sometimes used Aegir’s name to refer to the dangerous sea itself.

senses.definition: A dark green or black mineral of the pyroxene group, found in some igneous and metamorphic rocks.
examples: The rock sample contained crystals of aegirine and feldspar.
Aegirine often gives the stone its characteristic dark green color.

senses.definition: Plural of aegirine: crystals or grains of the dark green pyroxene mineral found in certain rocks.
examples: Under the microscope, fine aegirines could be seen in the thin section.
The geologist noted aegirines scattered through the matrix.

senses.definition: A mineral variety or rock containing both aegirine and another pyroxene such as augite, used in geological description.
examples: The intrusive body was classified as an aegirinolite because of its pyroxene content.
Aegirinolite can form in alkaline igneous environments.

senses.definition: Another form of aegirine, a dark green pyroxene mineral found in some igneous and metamorphic rocks.
examples: The thin section showed long needles of aegirite.
Aegirite occurs in many alkaline igneous rocks.

senses.definition: Plural of aegirite: crystals or grains of the mineral aegirine in rock samples.
examples: The rock is dotted with small aegirites visible to the naked eye.
Under polarized light, the aegirites show bright interference colors.
